title: Reading a CNN Architecture Diagram
concept: A CNN architecture diagram can be read step by step by tracking the input, each block or layer, the changing shapes, and the final head that produces class logits.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the classification head output at the very end of a CNN?

options.text: A reset network with no learned features

exercises.explain: Correct — the head turns the last feature maps into the final class scores. Now let's read an entire diagram from left to right.

cards.title: A CNN diagram is a travel map
cards.body: A CNN sketch can look like a lot of boxes and arrows. But if you read it like a map, it becomes manageable. Start at the input, follow the route through each operation, watch how the shape changes, and notice where the final classifier head takes over.

cards.title: How to read the diagram
cards.body: Read a simple CNN diagram from left to right: input -> feature blocks -> head -> outputs. At each step, ask two questions: what operation just happened, and what is the new shape? A label like 8x8x16 means height 8, width 8, and 16 channels stacked at each location.

cards.title: Like following stops on a subway map
cards.body: Each box in the diagram is like a station on a route. The data enters at one stop, gets transformed, then moves on. You do not need to memorize the whole map at once. You can follow the path one station at a time and still understand the journey.

cards.title: Read this toy CNN end to end
cards.body: Take this sketch: 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> 16x16x8 -> Conv(16) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> 8x8x16 -> GlobalAvgPool -> 16 -> Dense -> 3 logits. Read it in order: start with a 32x32 RGB image, extract 8 channels of features, halve the map with pooling, build 16 deeper channels, halve again, squeeze each channel to one number with global pooling, then let the dense layer output one score for each of the 3 classes.

cards.title: You do not need every detail at once
cards.body: Beginners sometimes think they must instantly decode every filter count, every activation, and every shape formula in one glance. You do not. A strong first read is simpler: follow the order, identify the main blocks, and track how space, channels, and the head change the data.

cards.title: What to track while reading
cards.body: A simple diagram becomes clear when you track both the operations and the shapes.

left.text:
Conv finds features
Pool shrinks space
Head outputs class scores

exercises.prompt: In the toy diagram 32x32x3 -> Conv(8) -> ReLU -> MaxPool -> 16x16x8, which step most directly halves the spatial size?
exercises.explain: Right — the pooling step is the one that most directly shrinks the map from 32x32 to 16x16 here.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In a CNN diagram, 8x8x16 usually means height 8, width 8, and 16 channels.
exercises.explain: True — that is the standard way to read a simple feature-map shape label.

exercises.sentence: When you read a CNN sketch, the arrows usually show the order data ___ through the model.
exercises.explain: Flows — the arrows tell you where the data goes next.

exercises.prompt: Which reading of this toy diagram is correct: 16x16x8 -> MaxPool -> 8x8x8 -> GlobalAvgPool -> 8 -> Dense -> 4 logits?

options.text: Pooling shrinks the spatial map, global average pooling squeezes each channel to one number, and the dense layer outputs 4 class scores
Global average pooling increases the image back to 16x16 before classification
The 4 logits are four separate images, not class scores

exercises.explain: Exactly — that is the correct end-to-end reading of the sketch.

exercises.prompt: You can now read a toy architecture like 32x32x3 -> block -> block -> global pool -> dense -> logits. What is the next big question that leads naturally into the next unit?

options.text: How much of the original image each deeper feature can actually 'see'
How to turn logits back into raw camera pixels
How to delete all spatial structure before the first convolution
Why CNNs should avoid using features altogether

exercises.explain: Exactly. Once you can read the boxes and arrows, the next deep question is about each feature's view of the original image — its receptive field. That is where we go next.
